activities.feedback: Reporting passive: 'Subject + is/are + reported/said/believed + to have + past participle.' Used for unverified or indirect information. Common in formal/academic writing.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les résultats du GCE auraient été publiés par le jury d'examen hier.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The GCE results are reported to have been released by the examination board yesterday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses the conditionnel ('auraient été') for reported/unverified information — structurally very different from English.

activities.question: By the time the Minister arrives in Maroua, all arrangements ___ .
activities.feedback: Future perfect passive: 'will + have + been + past participle.' It expresses completion before a future point. Active: 'They will have made all arrangements.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: D'ici l'arrivée du Ministre à Maroua, tous les préparatifs auront été faits.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: By the time the Minister arrives in Maroua, all arrangements will have been made .
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French future perfect passive: 'auront été + participe passé' — parallel structure.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The suspect is believed to escaped from the Kondengui prison last night.'
activities.answer: The suspect is believed to have escaped from the Kondengui prison last night.
activities.feedback: After 'is believed to,' use 'have + past participle' for past events: 'is believed to have escaped.' The infinitive alone ('to escape') would mean it hasn't happened yet.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le suspect aurait échappé de la prison de Kondengui la nuit dernière.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The suspect is believed to escaped from the Kondengui prison last night.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses conditionnel passé for reported information about past events.

activities.question: The cocoa ___ before it can be exported from the Douala port.
activities.feedback: 'Needs to be + past participle' is the full passive infinitive form. 'Needs processing' (gerund) is also correct but less formal. Both options convey the passive meaning.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le cacao doit être transformé avant de pouvoir être exporté du port de Douala.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The cocoa needs to be processed before it can be exported from the Douala port.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'doit être + participe passé' — a straightforward passive construction.

activities.question: It ___ that over 250 languages are spoken in Cameroon.
activities.feedback: Impersonal passive with 'It': 'It + is + past participle + that clause.' Common in academic and formal writing to present facts without naming the source.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: On estime que plus de 250 langues sont parlées au Cameroun.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: It is estimated that over 250 languages are spoken in Cameroon.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French often uses 'on + active verb' where English uses impersonal passive 'it is estimated.'

activities.question: The students demanded ___ about the schedule change in advance.
activities.feedback: 'Demand + to be + past participle.' The students want to receive the action (be informed), not perform it. This is a passive infinitive after a reporting verb.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les étudiants ont exigé d'être informés du changement d'emploi du temps à l'avance.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The students demanded to be informed about the schedule change in advance.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'd'être + participe passé' — the passive infinitive structure is similar.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The market in Mokolo is been renovated since January.'
activities.answer: The market in Mokolo has been being renovated since January.
activities.feedback: Present perfect continuous passive: 'has/have + been + being + past participle.' 'Since January' requires present perfect (continuous). Though this form is rare and often avoided, the correct structure uses 'has been being renovated.' In practice, 'has been under renovation' is preferred.
activities.correction: 'is been renovated' → 'has been being renovated' (or more naturally: 'has been under renovation')

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le marché de Mokolo est en rénovation depuis janvier.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The market in Mokolo is been renovated since January.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French avoids this complex passive — 'est en rénovation depuis' is simpler and preferred.

activities.question: The Anglophone teachers are said ___ on strike next week.
activities.feedback: 'Are said + to be + -ing' for reported future/planned actions. The continuous infinitive 'to be going' suggests the action is planned/imminent. 'To go' would be more general.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les enseignants anglophones feraient grève la semaine prochaine.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The Anglophone teachers are said to be going on strike next week.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses conditionnel ('feraient') for unverified reported future events.

activities.question: The candidates ___ not to use mobile phones during the Baccalauréat exam.
activities.feedback: Past simple passive: 'were + past participle.' 'Candidates' is plural, so 'were' is correct (not 'was'). Active: 'The invigilators instructed the candidates...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les candidats ont reçu l'instruction de ne pas utiliser de téléphones portables pendant l'examen du Baccalauréat.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The candidates were instructed not to use mobile phones during the Baccalauréat exam.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French often restructures passives into active constructions: 'ont reçu l'instruction' (received the instruction).

activities.question: The bridge over the Sanaga River ___ by the time the rainy season begins.
activities.feedback: Modal perfect passive: 'should + have + been + past participle.' Expresses expectation about a completed action before a deadline. Very common in project management language.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le pont sur le fleuve Sanaga devrait avoir été achevé avant le début de la saison des pluies.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The bridge over the Sanaga River should have been completed by the time the rainy season begins.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'devrait avoir été + participe passé' — structurally parallel to English.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The new bilingual school in Bertoua was building in 2019.'
activities.answer: The new bilingual school in Bertoua was built in 2019.
activities.feedback: Past simple passive requires 'was/were + PAST PARTICIPLE,' not present participle. 'Was building' is active past continuous (the school was building something). 'Was built' is the correct passive.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: La nouvelle école bilingue de Bertoua a été construite en 2019.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The new bilingual school in Bertoua was building in 2019.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French past passive: 'a été + participe passé' (passé composé passive).

activities.question: The President is known ___ several foreign languages fluently.
activities.feedback: 'Is known + to + infinitive' for current facts. The President actively speaks (not 'to be spoken'). Use 'to have + past participle' only for past completed actions.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le Président est connu pour parler couramment plusieurs langues étrangères.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The President is known to speak several foreign languages fluently.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'est connu pour + infinitif' — 'pour' replaces 'to.'

activities.question: Having ___ of the danger, the villagers near Mount Cameroon evacuated immediately.
activities.feedback: Perfect participle passive: 'Having + been + past participle.' Shows the passive action happened before the main verb. The villagers received the warning (passive), then evacuated.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Ayant été prévenus du danger, les villageois près du Mont Cameroun ont évacué immédiatement.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Having been warned of the danger, the villagers near Mount Cameroon evacuated immediately.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French mirrors this perfectly: 'ayant été + participe passé.'

activities.question: The proposal for a new national language policy ___ currently ___ by a parliamentary committee.
activities.feedback: Present continuous passive with split verb: 'is + currently + being + past participle.' The adverb 'currently' goes between 'is' and 'being.' The proposal receives the review (passive).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: La proposition pour une nouvelle politique linguistique nationale est actuellement examinée par une commission parlementaire.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The proposal for a new national language policy is ... being reviewed currently is ... being reviewed by a parliamentary committee.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French doesn't need 'being' — 'est actuellement examinée' suffices for the ongoing passive.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The children were been taken care of by their grandmother in Bafoussam.'
activities.answer: The children were being taken care of by their grandmother in Bafoussam.
activities.feedback: Past continuous passive: 'were + BEING + past participle,' not 'been.' 'Been' is for perfect tenses (have been). 'Being' is for continuous tenses (is/was being).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les enfants étaient pris en charge par leur grand-mère à Bafoussam.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The children were been taken care of by their grandmother in Bafoussam.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses imparfait passive: 'étaient + participe passé' — no need for a 'being' equivalent.

activities.question: The footballer from Cameroon ___ to have been offered a contract by a European club.
activities.feedback: Reporting passive with 'to have been + past participle' for completed past action: 'is rumoured to have been offered.' The offer happened before the rumour.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le footballeur camerounais aurait reçu une offre de contrat d'un club européen.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The footballer from Cameroon is rumoured to have been offered a contract by a European club.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French conditionnel passé ('aurait reçu') handles both the passive and the reported/uncertain aspect.

activities.question: The old colonial buildings in Buea deserve ___ as national heritage sites.
activities.feedback: 'Deserve + to be + past participle' — the buildings receive preservation (passive). 'Deserve preserving' is also grammatically acceptable but less common in formal writing.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les anciens bâtiments coloniaux de Buea méritent d'être préservés en tant que sites du patrimoine national.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The old colonial buildings in Buea deserve to be preserved as national heritage sites.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'méritent d'être + participe passé' — very similar structure.

activities.question: Not much ___ about the impact of climate change on Lake Chad until recently.
activities.feedback: Past simple passive: 'was + past participle.' 'Not much' is the subject receiving the action of 'knowing.' Active: 'People didn't know much about...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: On ne savait pas grand-chose sur l'impact du changement climatique sur le lac Tchad jusqu'à récemment.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Not much was known about the impact of climate change on Lake Chad until recently.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French prefers active 'on ne savait pas' over passive in this context.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The ceremony was attended with over 500 guests from across Central Africa.'
activities.answer: The ceremony was attended by over 500 guests from across Central Africa.
activities.feedback: In passive voice, the agent (doer) is introduced by 'by,' not 'with.' 'With' indicates instrument or accompaniment, not the agent of the action.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: La cérémonie a été suivie par plus de 500 invités de toute l'Afrique centrale.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The ceremony was attended with over 500 guests from across Central Africa.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'par' for the agent in passive — equivalent to English 'by.' The error of using 'avec' (with) instead of 'par' (by) also occurs in French learners.

activities.question: The teacher told the students that they ___ their homework by Friday.
activities.feedback: In reported speech, 'must' becomes 'had to' (backshift). Direct: 'You must submit your homework.' → Reported: 'She told them they had to submit...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le professeur a dit aux élèves qu'ils devaient rendre leurs devoirs avant vendredi.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The teacher told the students that they had to submit their homework by Friday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French also backshifts: 'doivent' (présent) → 'devaient' (imparfait) in reported speech.

activities.question: The governor announced that a new hospital ___ in Garoua the following year.
activities.feedback: 'Will' becomes 'would' in reported speech (backshift). 'Next year' becomes 'the following year.' Direct: 'A hospital will be built next year.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le gouverneur a annoncé qu'un nouvel hôpital serait construit à Garoua l'année suivante.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The governor announced that a new hospital would be built in Garoua the following year.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: futur ('sera') → conditionnel présent ('serait') — same backshift principle.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'She asked me where do I come from in Cameroon.'
activities.answer: She asked me where I came from in Cameroon.
activities.feedback: Two errors: (1) Reported questions use statement word order, not question order — 'where I came,' not 'where do I come.' (2) Backshift: 'come' → 'came.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle m'a demandé d'où je venais au Cameroun.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'She asked me where do I come from in Cameroon.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French reported questions also use statement order and backshift: 'viens' → 'venais.'

activities.question: The doctor advised the patient ___ more water and rest for a week.
activities.feedback: 'Advise + object + to + infinitive' is the standard pattern for reported advice. Direct: 'Drink more water.' → 'The doctor advised him to drink more water.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le médecin a conseillé au patient de boire plus d'eau et de se reposer pendant une semaine.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The doctor advised the patient to drink more water and rest for a week.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'conseiller à qqn de + infinitif' — similar infinitive construction.

activities.question: The headmaster insisted that every student ___ the school uniform properly.
activities.feedback: 'Insist that + subject + base verb (subjunctive).' No '-s' even with third-person singular. This is the mandative subjunctive, common after verbs like insist, demand, suggest, recommend.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le directeur a insisté pour que chaque élève porte l'uniforme scolaire correctement.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The headmaster insisted that every student wear the school uniform properly.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'insister pour que + subjonctif' — both languages use subjunctive here, but it's more visible in French ('porte' subj. = 'porte' ind.).

activities.question: My colleague mentioned that she ___ to Limbe the previous weekend.
activities.feedback: Past simple → past perfect in reported speech. Direct: 'I travelled to Limbe last weekend.' 'Last weekend' → 'the previous weekend.' 'Travelled' → 'had travelled.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Ma collègue a mentionné qu'elle s'était rendue à Limbe le week-end précédent.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: My colleague mentioned that she had travelled to Limbe the previous weekend.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: passé composé ('s'est rendue') → plus-que-parfait ('s'était rendue') in reported speech.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The manager warned the employees to not arrive late again.'
activities.answer: The manager warned the employees not to arrive late again.
activities.feedback: In reported speech with infinitives, 'not' goes BEFORE 'to': 'not to arrive,' not 'to not arrive.' (Though 'to not arrive' is increasingly accepted informally, the standard rule is 'not to.')

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le directeur a averti les employés de ne pas arriver en retard à nouveau.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The manager warned the employees to not arrive late again.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'de ne pas + infinitif' — negation wraps around 'de' similarly: 'de ne pas arriver.'

activities.question: The witness denied ___ at the scene of the accident in Bamenda.
activities.feedback: 'Deny + gerund/having + past participle.' For a past action: 'deny having been' (perfect gerund). 'Deny' is always followed by a gerund, never an infinitive.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le témoin a nié avoir été sur les lieux de l'accident à Bamenda.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The witness denied having been at the scene of the accident in Bamenda.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'nier + infinitif passé' (avoir été) — uses infinitive where English uses gerund.

activities.question: The journalist reported that the flood ___ over 200 homes in the Far North region.
activities.feedback: Past simple → past perfect backshift. The destruction happened before the reporting. Direct: 'The flood destroyed over 200 homes.' → 'had destroyed.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le journaliste a rapporté que l'inondation avait détruit plus de 200 maisons dans la région de l'Extrême-Nord.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The journalist reported that the flood had destroyed over 200 homes in the Far North region.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: passé composé → plus-que-parfait in reported speech, same as English.

activities.question: The committee recommended that the project ___ immediately.
activities.feedback: 'Recommend that + subject + base verb (subjunctive).' Passive subjunctive: 'be started' (not 'is started'). 'Should be started' is the British alternative.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le comité a recommandé que le projet soit lancé immédiatement.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The committee recommended that the project be started immediately.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'recommander que + subjonctif' — 'soit lancé' clearly shows subjunctive form.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'He asked me that if I had ever visited the Waza National Park.'
activities.answer: He asked me if I had ever visited the Waza National Park.
activities.feedback: Don't use 'that' with reported yes/no questions. Use 'if' or 'whether' alone: 'He asked me if...' The word 'that' introduces reported statements, not questions.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il m'a demandé si j'avais déjà visité le Parc National de Waza.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'He asked me that if I had ever visited the Waza National Park.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'si' for reported yes/no questions — never combines 'que' and 'si.'

activities.question: The minister suggested that Cameroon ___ its renewable energy sector.
activities.feedback: 'Suggest that + subject + base verb (subjunctive).' No '-s' for third person, no 'should' needed (American English). British English accepts 'should develop.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le ministre a suggéré que le Cameroun développe son secteur des énergies renouvelables.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The minister suggested that Cameroon develop its renewable energy sector.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'suggérer que + subjonctif' — 'développe' is subjunctive (same form as indicative here).

activities.question: She complained about ___ kept waiting at the préfecture for three hours.
activities.feedback: 'Complain about + having been + past participle' for a past passive experience. The perfect gerund ('having been') shows the waiting happened before the complaining.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle s'est plainte d'avoir été fait attendre à la préfecture pendant trois heures.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She complained about having been kept waiting at the préfecture for three hours.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'se plaindre de + infinitif passé' (d'avoir été) — infinitive where English uses gerund.

activities.question: The parents wanted to know whether their children ___ well at the bilingual school.
activities.feedback: Present continuous → past continuous in reported speech. Direct: 'Are our children doing well?' → 'whether their children were doing well.' 'Whether' introduces reported yes/no questions.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les parents voulaient savoir si leurs enfants se débrouillaient bien à l'école bilingue.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The parents wanted to know whether their children were doing well at the bilingual school.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'vouloir savoir si + imparfait' — imparfait replaces présent in reported speech.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'The director told to the staff that the office would close early on Friday.'
activities.answer: The director told the staff that the office would close early on Friday.
activities.feedback: 'Tell' takes a direct object WITHOUT 'to': 'told the staff' (not 'told to the staff'). Compare with 'say': 'said to the staff.' This is one of the most common errors with reported speech.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le directeur a dit au personnel que le bureau fermerait tôt vendredi.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'The director told to the staff that the office would close early on Friday.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'dire à qqn' always uses 'à' — this is why French speakers often add 'to' after 'tell' in English.

activities.question: He wondered how many people ___ the Unity Day celebrations in Buea that year.
activities.feedback: 'Wondered' (past) triggers backshift. Direct: 'How many people attended?' → past perfect 'had attended.' 'That year' replaces 'this year.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il se demandait combien de personnes avaient assisté aux célébrations de la Fête de l'Unité à Buea cette année-là.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He wondered how many people had attended the Unity Day celebrations in Buea that year.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: passé composé → plus-que-parfait, 'cette année' → 'cette année-là.'

activities.question: The chairperson proposed that the meeting ___ to the following Monday.
activities.feedback: 'Propose that + subject + base verb (subjunctive).' Passive subjunctive: 'be postponed.' Time shift: 'next Monday' → 'the following Monday.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le président a proposé que la réunion soit reportée au lundi suivant.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The chairperson proposed that the meeting be postponed to the following Monday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'proposer que + subjonctif' — 'soit reportée' clearly subjunctive.

activities.question: Find and correct the error: 'She said me that she was planning to open a boutique in Douala.'
activities.answer: She told me that she was planning to open a boutique in Douala.
activities.feedback: 'Say' doesn't take a direct personal object. Either: 'She said (that) she was...' OR 'She told me (that) she was...' Never 'She said me.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle m'a dit qu'elle prévoyait d'ouvrir une boutique à Douala.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Find and correct the error: 'She said me that she was planning to open a boutique in Douala.'
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'dire à qqn' always uses indirect object — this is why the error 'said me' is common among French speakers.

activities.question: The professor urged that every thesis ___ checked for plagiarism before submission.
activities.feedback: 'Urge that + subject + base verb (subjunctive)': 'that every thesis BE checked.' The subjunctive 'be' is invariable — same for all persons. This is the mandative subjunctive.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le professeur a insisté pour que chaque thèse soit vérifiée pour plagiat avant soumission.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The professor urged that every thesis be checked for plagiarism before submission.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'insister pour que + subjonctif' — 'soit vérifiée' is clearly subjunctive.

activities.question: The refugees explained that they ___ their village three days before because of the fighting.
activities.feedback: Past simple → past perfect. Direct: 'We left our village three days ago.' → 'had left...three days before.' Time shift: 'ago' → 'before/earlier/previously.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les réfugiés ont expliqué qu'ils avaient quitté leur village trois jours auparavant à cause des combats.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The refugees explained that they had left their village three days before because of the fighting.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French: 'il y a' → 'auparavant/avant' in reported speech. Passé composé → plus-que-parfait.

activities.question: By the time we arrived at the Douala airport, the plane ___.
activities.feedback: Past perfect ('had + past participle') for an action completed before another past action. The plane left BEFORE we arrived.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: L'avion était déjà parti quand nous sommes arrivés.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The plane had already left when we arrived.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses plus-que-parfait similarly, but students often use passé composé instead.

activities.question: She ___ in Bamenda for ten years before she moved to Douala.
activities.feedback: Past perfect for duration before another past event. 'For ten years' shows duration, 'before she moved' establishes the past reference point.

activities.question: The children ___ their homework before their father came home from the ministry.
activities.feedback: Past perfect: homework was done FIRST, then father arrived. Two completed past actions in sequence require past perfect for the earlier one.

activities.question: I didn't recognize him because he ___ a lot since secondary school.
activities.feedback: Past perfect explains the cause of a past situation. He changed (earlier) → I didn't recognize (later). Both are past, so the earlier one takes past perfect.

activities.question: After the students ___ the national anthem, the ceremony began.
activities.feedback: 'After' + past perfect shows the first action was fully completed before the second began. 'Sung' is the past participle of 'sing.'

activities.question: We couldn't enter the stadium because we ___ our tickets at home.
activities.feedback: Past perfect for cause-and-effect in the past. Leaving tickets (earlier action) caused not entering (later action).

activities.question: The teacher asked if anyone ___ the chapter before class.
activities.feedback: Reported speech with past perfect: the reading should have happened before the asking. 'Asked' (past) shifts 'have read' to 'had read.'

activities.question: If only I ___ harder for the GCE, I would have passed.
activities.feedback: Third conditional / past regret: 'If only + past perfect' for unreal past. The studying didn't happen, and the speaker regrets it.

activities.question: No sooner ___ the house than it started raining heavily in Kribi.
activities.feedback: 'No sooner + had + subject + past participle + than...' is a formal structure with inversion. It means 'immediately after we left, it rained.'

activities.question: The market women ___ all the plantains before midday.
activities.feedback: Past perfect with 'before' emphasizes completion before a time reference. All plantains were gone by midday.

activities.question: By next December, I ___ from the University of Yaoundé.
activities.feedback: Future perfect ('will + have + past participle') for actions completed before a future deadline. 'By next December' sets the future reference point.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: D'ici décembre prochain, j'aurai obtenu mon diplôme.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: By next December, I will have graduated.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French futur antérieur ('aurai obtenu') maps well, but students often use simple future instead.

activities.question: By the time you arrive in Maroua, we ___ dinner already.
activities.feedback: 'By the time' + present simple triggers future perfect in the main clause. Dinner will be finished before your arrival.

activities.question: She ___ in the hospital for 20 years by the time she retires next March.
activities.feedback: Future perfect for duration leading up to a future point. 'For 20 years' + 'by the time she retires' = accumulated time before a future event.

activities.question: The construction workers ___ the bridge before the rainy season starts.
activities.feedback: Future perfect shows completion before a future time. The bridge will be done before the rains come.

activities.question: By 2030, Cameroon ___ its population to over 30 million.
activities.feedback: 'By 2030' is a future deadline → future perfect. The population growth will be a completed fact by that year.

activities.question: I'm sure they ___ the results by the time we check tomorrow.
activities.feedback: 'By the time we check' sets a future reference → results will already be out (completed before checking). Future perfect is required.

activities.question: Don't call at 9 PM — the children ___ to bed by then.
activities.feedback: 'By then' (by 9 PM) signals completion before a future point. The children's bedtime will already have passed.

activities.question: Next month, my parents ___ married for 25 years.
activities.feedback: Future perfect for a state that accumulates to a future milestone. 'For 25 years' + 'next month' = duration reaching a future point.

activities.question: The train ___ by the time we get to the Douala station if we don't hurry.
activities.feedback: 'By the time we get' + conditional urgency → future perfect. The departure will be complete before our arrival.

activities.question: By the end of this semester, the professor ___ every chapter of the textbook.
activities.feedback: 'By the end of this semester' → future perfect. All chapters will be done by that future deadline.

activities.question: Neither the teacher nor the students ___ aware of the schedule change.
activities.feedback: With 'neither...nor,' the verb agrees with the NEAREST subject. 'Students' (plural) is nearest → 'were.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Ni le professeur ni les élèves n'étaient au courant.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Neither the teacher nor the students were aware.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'ni...ni' with plural verb regardless of proximity. English proximity rule is unique.

activities.question: Each of the students in the class ___ required to take the exam.
activities.feedback: 'Each' is always singular, even when followed by 'of + plural noun.' The verb must be singular: 'each IS required.'

activities.question: The news about the Indomitable Lions ___ very exciting.
activities.feedback: 'News' looks plural but is uncountable/singular in English. Always: 'the news IS.' Other examples: mathematics, physics, economics.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les nouvelles sur les Lions Indomptables sont très excitantes.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The news about the Indomitable Lions is very exciting.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'les nouvelles' is plural, so French speakers naturally use 'are' with 'news' in English.

activities.question: The number of students enrolled at the university ___ increased this year.
activities.feedback: 'THE number of' = singular (refers to the number itself). 'A number of' = plural (means 'many'). Here: 'the number HAS increased.'

activities.question: Mathematics ___ my favourite subject at the bilingual school.
activities.feedback: Subjects ending in '-s' that look plural but are singular: mathematics, physics, economics, politics (when meaning the subject).

activities.question: Not only the principal but also the teachers ___ present at the ceremony.
activities.feedback: 'Not only...but also': verb agrees with the subject after 'but also.' 'Teachers' is plural → 'were.' Same proximity rule as 'neither...nor.'

activities.question: There ___ several reasons why the project in Kribi was delayed.
activities.feedback: In 'there is/are' sentences, the verb agrees with the real subject that follows. 'Several reasons' is plural → 'there WERE.'

activities.question: Everyone in the village ___ the old chief very well.
activities.feedback: 'Everyone' is always singular, even though it refers to multiple people. Always: 'everyone KNOWS/IS/HAS.'

activities.question: A large number of tourists ___ the national parks in Cameroon every year.
activities.feedback: 'A number of' = plural (means 'many'). Contrast with 'THE number of' = singular. 'A number of tourists VISIT.'

activities.question: The committee ___ divided on the issue of bilingual education reform.
activities.feedback: Collective nouns (committee, team, family) are usually singular in American English: 'the committee IS.' British English may use plural when emphasizing individual members.

activities.question: She wants to become ___ engineer after finishing university.
activities.feedback: 'An' before vowel sounds: 'an engineer.' We use a/an for professions after 'become': 'become an engineer.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle veut devenir ingénieur.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She wants to become an engineer.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French drops the article after 'devenir' for professions. English requires 'a/an.'

activities.question: My father is ___ doctor at the Yaoundé Central Hospital.
activities.feedback: English requires 'a/an' before professions: 'He is A doctor.' French: 'Il est médecin' (no article).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Mon père est médecin à l'hôpital central de Yaoundé.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: My father is a doctor at Yaoundé Central Hospital.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French omits the article with professions after 'être.' This is the #1 cause of article errors for French speakers.

activities.question: ___ Mount Cameroon is the highest peak in West Africa.
activities.feedback: No article before single mountain names: 'Mount Cameroon,' 'Mount Kilimanjaro.' But: 'THE Alps,' 'THE Himalayas' (ranges).

activities.question: We went swimming in ___ Atlantic Ocean during our holiday in Kribi.
activities.feedback: Use 'the' with oceans, seas, and rivers: 'THE Atlantic Ocean,' 'THE Sanaga River.' But NOT with lakes: 'Lake Chad' (no 'the').

activities.question: I usually have ___ breakfast at 7 o'clock before going to school.
activities.feedback: No article before meal names in general: 'have breakfast/lunch/dinner.' But: 'THE breakfast we had was delicious' (specific one).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Je prends le petit-déjeuner à 7 heures.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: I have breakfast at 7 o'clock.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'le' with meals ('le petit-déjeuner'). English uses no article for general meals.

activities.question: ___ water in this well is not safe to drink.
activities.feedback: 'The' for specific water (in THIS well). General water: 'Water is essential for life' (no article).

activities.question: He plays ___ football every Saturday at the Omnisports Stadium.
activities.feedback: No article before sport names: 'play football/basketball/tennis.' But: 'play THE guitar/piano' (musical instruments need 'the').

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il joue au football chaque samedi.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He plays football every Saturday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'au' (à + le) with sports. English uses no article. Students often say 'play the football.'

activities.question: She is ___ most intelligent student in our class.
activities.feedback: Always use 'the' with superlatives: 'THE most intelligent,' 'THE best,' 'THE tallest.' This is one rule French and English share.

activities.question: Could you pass me ___ salt, please?
activities.feedback: 'The' for specific items in context — the salt that is on the table. Both speakers know which salt is meant.

activities.question: I bought ___ umbrella because it was raining in Douala.
activities.feedback: 'An' before vowel sounds: 'an umbrella.' First mention of an unspecified item → 'a/an.'

activities.question: ___ honesty is the best policy, my grandmother always says.
activities.feedback: No article before abstract nouns used in general: 'Honesty,' 'Love,' 'Education.' But: 'THE honesty he showed was admirable' (specific).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: L'honnêteté est la meilleure politique.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Honesty is the best policy.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French ALWAYS uses an article with abstract nouns ('l'honnêteté'). English drops it for general statements.

activities.question: My sister is studying at ___ University of Douala.
activities.feedback: 'The' with universities that use 'of': 'THE University of Douala.' But: no article for university names without 'of': 'Harvard University.'

activities.question: I have ___ headache. Can I go to the school nurse?
activities.feedback: 'A' with singular countable ailments: 'a headache,' 'a cold,' 'a fever.' But: 'I have flu' (no article, uncountable in British English).

activities.question: ___ rich should help ___ poor, my pastor says.
activities.feedback: 'The + adjective' = a group of people: 'the rich' (rich people), 'the poor' (poor people), 'the elderly.' Always plural meaning.

activities.question: She arrived at ___ Douala International Airport at midnight.
activities.feedback: No article before airports, stations, and parks named after people/places: 'Douala International Airport,' 'Heathrow Airport.' But: 'THE airport' (generic).

activities.question: ___ English is spoken in the North West and South West regions.
activities.feedback: No article before language names: 'English,' 'French,' 'Pidgin.' But: 'THE English spoken in Cameroon is different' (specific variety).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: L'anglais est parlé dans les régions du Nord-Ouest et du Sud-Ouest.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: English is spoken in the North West and South West.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French always uses 'l'' before languages. English never uses 'the' before languages in general.

activities.question: What ___ beautiful country Cameroon is!
activities.feedback: In exclamations with 'what': 'What + a/an + adjective + singular noun!' 'What a beautiful country!' 'What an amazing city!'

activities.question: He goes to ___ church every Sunday with his family in Bamenda.
activities.feedback: No article with institutions when used for their primary purpose: 'go to church' (to pray), 'go to school' (to study). But: 'go to THE church' (the building).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il va à l'église chaque dimanche.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He goes to church every Sunday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French always uses 'à l'église.' English drops the article when referring to the activity, not the building.

activities.question: I need to buy ___ new pair of shoes for the ceremony.
activities.feedback: 'A' before consonant sounds: 'a new pair.' First mention, unspecified → indefinite article 'a.'

activities.question: ___ Lake Chad has been shrinking over the past decades.
activities.feedback: No article before lake names: 'Lake Chad,' 'Lake Victoria.' But: 'THE Great Lakes,' 'THE Caspian Sea' (seas use 'the').

activities.question: The ___ in the Waza National Park are protected by law.
activities.feedback: 'Wildlife' is uncountable — no plural form. French 'la faune' is also singular, so this is a rare case where both languages agree.

activities.question: The ___ gave us a lot of useful ___ about the new curriculum.
activities.feedback: 'Information' is uncountable in English — never 'informations.' French 'des informations' is countable, causing frequent errors.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les professeurs nous ont donné beaucoup d'informations.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The teachers gave us a lot of information.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'information' is countable (des informations). English 'information' is always uncountable.

activities.question: She bought two ___ of bread at the bakery in Yaoundé.
activities.feedback: Nouns ending in '-f' or '-fe' change to '-ves': loaf→loaves, knife→knives, wife→wives. Exception: roofs, chiefs.

activities.question: Several ___ were playing near the river in the village.
activities.feedback: 'Children' is the irregular plural of 'child.' Never 'childs' or 'childrens.' Other irregulars: man→men, woman→women, tooth→teeth.

activities.question: I need to buy new ___ for the rainy season in Douala.
activities.feedback: 'Furniture' is uncountable in English. Say 'some furniture' or 'pieces of furniture,' never 'furnitures.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: J'ai besoin d'acheter de nouveaux meubles.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: I need to buy new furniture.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'meuble/meubles' is countable. English 'furniture' is always uncountable — a major source of errors.

activities.question: The fishermen caught many ___ in the Sanaga River.
activities.feedback: 'Fish' has the same singular and plural form (like 'sheep' and 'deer'). 'Fishes' exists only when referring to different species.

activities.question: The professor asked us to read two ___ of the research paper.
activities.feedback: Greek-origin plurals: analysis→analyses, thesis→theses, crisis→crises, basis→bases. The '-is' ending changes to '-es.'

activities.question: We need more ___ to finish building the school in the village.
activities.feedback: 'Equipment' is uncountable. Say 'pieces of equipment' or 'some equipment.' Never 'equipments.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Nous avons besoin de plus d'équipements.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: We need more equipment.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'équipement' can be plural ('des équipements'). English 'equipment' is always uncountable.

activities.question: The two ___ discussed the case at the courthouse in Buea.
activities.feedback: Nouns ending in vowel + y: just add -s (attorney→attorneys, monkey→monkeys). Only consonant + y changes to -ies (city→cities).

activities.question: Her ___ about travelling to all ten regions of Cameroon were very ambitious.
activities.feedback: Regular plural: just add -s. 'Plans' is plural. Don't confuse the plural -s with the possessive 's (apostrophe).

activities.question: She ___ to the market yesterday.
activities.feedback: Frequency adverbs ('always, often, never') go BEFORE the main verb: 'always goes.' In French, adverbs often go after the verb, causing word order errors.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle va toujours tôt au marché.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She always goes early to the market.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French puts 'toujours' after the verb. English puts 'always' before the main verb.

activities.question: He speaks ___.
activities.feedback: Adverbs of manner go AFTER the object: 'speaks English fluently.' Never between verb and object in English.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il parle couramment anglais.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He speaks English fluently.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French puts the adverb between verb and object ('parle couramment anglais'). English puts it after the object.

activities.question: I have ___ been to the Waza National Park.
activities.feedback: 'Never' goes between auxiliary and main verb: 'have NEVER been.' It already contains negation, so no 'not' needed.

activities.question: The children were playing ___ in the schoolyard.
activities.feedback: When multiple adverbs follow a verb: manner (how) comes before place/company. 'Happily' (manner) before 'together' (company).

activities.question: Not only ___ hard, but she also volunteers at the community centre.
activities.feedback: 'Not only' at the start of a clause triggers inversion: 'Not only DOES SHE study.' Other triggers: 'never,' 'rarely,' 'seldom,' 'hardly.'

activities.question: She gave ___ as a gift for his birthday.
activities.feedback: Two patterns: 'gave [person] [thing]' or 'gave [thing] to [person].' 'Gave her brother a phone' = indirect object first.

activities.question: Do you know ___?
options: where is it the post office
activities.feedback: Embedded questions use STATEMENT order: 'where the post office IS' (not 'where IS the post office'). The inversion happens in direct questions only.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Savez-vous où se trouve la poste ?
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Do you know where the post office is?
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French can invert in embedded questions ('où se trouve'). English must use statement order in embedded questions.

activities.question: She ___ drives to work in Douala because of the traffic.
activities.feedback: Frequency adverbs go BEFORE the main verb: 'She RARELY drives.' After 'be': 'She is rarely late.' Use the adverb form 'rarely' (not adjective 'rare').

activities.question: Seldom ___ such a talented musician in our village.
activities.feedback: 'Seldom' at the start triggers subject-auxiliary inversion: 'Seldom HAVE I seen.' This is formal/literary English.

activities.question: She asked me ___ the meeting would start.
activities.feedback: 'What time' is the natural English question phrase. 'At what time' is overly formal. 'What hour' is a French calque from 'quelle heure.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle m'a demandé à quelle heure la réunion commencerait.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She asked me what time the meeting would start.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'à quelle heure' translates literally as 'at what hour' — a common calque error. English uses 'what time.'

activities.question: She is very good ___ mathematics at her school in Bamenda.
activities.feedback: 'Good AT' a subject/skill. French uses 'bon EN' — this is a frequent source of errors.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle est très bonne en mathématiques.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She is very good at mathematics.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'en' where English uses 'at.' Students often say 'good in' by calque from 'bon en.'

activities.question: We arrived ___ Douala after a long journey from Maroua.
activities.feedback: 'Arrive IN' a city/country (large place). 'Arrive AT' a building/small place. Never 'arrive TO.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Nous sommes arrivés à Douala.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: We arrived in Douala.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'arriver à' for all places. English distinguishes 'arrive in' (city) vs 'arrive at' (building).

activities.question: He depends ___ his older brother for school fees.
activities.feedback: 'Depend ON' someone/something. French uses 'dépendre DE' — the preposition doesn't match.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il dépend de son frère aîné.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He depends on his older brother.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'dépendre de' → students say 'depend of' instead of 'depend on.'

activities.question: I'm interested ___ learning more about Cameroonian history.
activities.feedback: 'Interested IN' something. French uses 'intéressé PAR' — another preposition mismatch.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Je suis intéressé par l'histoire camerounaise.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: I'm interested in Cameroonian history.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'par' (by). Students often say 'interested by' instead of 'interested in.'

activities.question: The ceremony will take place ___ the 20th of May.
activities.feedback: 'ON' + specific date: 'on the 20th of May,' 'on Monday.' 'IN' + month/year: 'in May.' 'AT' + time: 'at 3 PM.'

activities.question: She was angry ___ her classmate for copying her homework.
activities.feedback: 'Angry WITH' a person, 'angry ABOUT/AT' a situation. French uses 'fâché CONTRE' — yet another mismatch.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle était fâchée contre son camarade.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She was angry with her classmate.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'contre' (against) doesn't map to 'with.' Students often say 'angry against.'

activities.question: The children are afraid ___ the dark during the power cuts.
activities.feedback: 'Afraid OF' something. This matches French 'avoir peur DE,' so it's one of the easier preposition combinations.

activities.question: He apologized ___ being late to the meeting.
activities.feedback: 'Apologize FOR' + gerund/noun. 'Apologize TO' someone. French uses 's'excuser DE,' causing errors with the preposition choice.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il s'est excusé d'être en retard.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He apologized for being late.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 's'excuser de' → students say 'apologize of' instead of 'apologize for.'

activities.question: We are looking forward ___ the holidays.
activities.feedback: 'Look forward TO' + noun/gerund. Note: 'to' here is a preposition (not infinitive), so it's followed by -ing: 'looking forward to SEEING you.'

activities.question: The students complained ___ the poor quality of the food in the cafeteria.
activities.feedback: 'Complain ABOUT' something. 'Complain TO' someone. French uses 'se plaindre DE,' leading to 'complain of' (which is formal/old-fashioned).

activities.question: She dreams ___ becoming a pilot one day.
activities.feedback: 'Dream OF' + gerund (aspiration). 'Dream ABOUT' (during sleep). French uses 'rêver DE' for both meanings.

activities.question: He insisted ___ paying for everyone's meal at the restaurant.
activities.feedback: 'Insist ON' + gerund. French uses 'insister POUR,' so students often say 'insist for.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il a insisté pour payer le repas de tout le monde.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He insisted on paying for everyone's meal.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'insister pour' → students say 'insist for' instead of 'insist on.'

activities.question: The plane arrived ___ the airport two hours late.
activities.feedback: 'Arrive AT' a specific place/building (airport, station, office). 'Arrive IN' a city/country. Never 'arrive TO.'

activities.question: She is married ___ a businessman from Bafoussam.
activities.feedback: 'Married TO' someone (not 'married with'). French 'marié AVEC' directly causes the error 'married with.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle est mariée avec un homme d'affaires de Bafoussam.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She is married to a businessman from Bafoussam.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'marié avec' → English 'married to.' This is one of the most persistent preposition errors for French speakers.

activities.question: The report consists ___ three main sections.
activities.feedback: 'Consist OF' (= be made up of). Don't confuse with 'consist IN' (= have as essential feature, very formal). French 'consister EN/À' doesn't help.

activities.question: He succeeded ___ passing the entrance exam to the university.
activities.feedback: 'Succeed IN' + gerund. French uses 'réussir À,' leading to 'succeed to' — a common error.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il a réussi à passer l'examen d'entrée.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He succeeded in passing the entrance exam.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'réussir à' → students say 'succeed to' instead of 'succeed in.'

activities.question: The match will be played ___ Saturday afternoon.
activities.feedback: 'ON' + day of the week or specific day part: 'on Saturday,' 'on Saturday afternoon.' 'IN the afternoon' (general), but 'ON Saturday afternoon' (specific day).

activities.question: She is responsible ___ organizing the school's cultural festival.
activities.feedback: 'Responsible FOR' + noun/gerund. French uses 'responsable DE,' so students often say 'responsible of.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle est responsable de l'organisation du festival culturel.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She is responsible for organizing the cultural festival.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'responsable de' → students say 'responsible of' instead of 'responsible for.'

activities.question: I'm not used ___ waking up so early for school.
activities.feedback: 'Be used TO' + gerund (= be accustomed to). Note: 'to' is a preposition here, followed by -ing. Don't confuse with 'used to' + infinitive (past habit).

activities.question: The decision was made ___ behalf ___ the entire community.
activities.feedback: 'On behalf OF' = representing someone. Fixed expression. French 'au nom DE' maps partially but uses different prepositions.

activities.question: You ___ drive carefully on the Douala-Yaoundé highway; there are many accidents.
activities.feedback: 'Must' for strong obligation/necessity. 'Can' = ability, 'might/could' = possibility. The danger makes 'must' the right choice.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Tu dois conduire prudemment sur l'autoroute Douala-Yaoundé.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: You must drive carefully on the Douala-Yaoundé highway.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'devoir' covers both 'must' and 'have to.' English distinguishes internal obligation (must) from external (have to).

activities.question: You ___ take an umbrella. It looks like it's going to rain in Douala.
activities.feedback: 'Should' for advice/recommendation. 'Must' would be too strong (obligation). 'Might' is about possibility, not advice.

activities.question: The electricity ___ go off at any moment during the dry season.
activities.feedback: 'Might' for uncertain possibility. 'Must' = obligation/certainty. 'Will' = certainty about the future. 'Might' captures the unpredictability.

activities.question: Students ___ use their phones during exams. It's strictly forbidden.
activities.feedback: 'Must not' = prohibition (forbidden). 'Don't have to' = no obligation (optional). These are very different! 'Strictly forbidden' = must not.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les étudiants ne doivent pas utiliser leurs téléphones.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Students must not use their phones.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'ne pas devoir' maps to 'must not.' But 'don't have to' (no obligation) has no direct French equivalent, causing confusion.

activities.question: She ___ speak Pidgin, French, and English — she's truly multilingual.
activities.feedback: 'Can' for ability. She has the skill to speak three languages. 'May' is for permission or possibility, not ability.

activities.question: You ___ wear a uniform to the office — casual clothes are fine.
activities.feedback: 'Don't have to' = no obligation (you can choose). 'Must not' = forbidden. The context ('casual clothes are fine') shows it's optional, not forbidden.

activities.question: She ___ have left already; the lights in her house are off.
activities.feedback: 'Must have + past participle' for logical deduction about the past. The evidence (lights off) leads to the conclusion (she left). Near-certainty.

activities.question: He ___ have told you the truth. He's known for lying.
activities.feedback: 'Can't have + past participle' for negative deduction about the past. The evidence (he lies) makes it near-impossible that he told the truth.

activities.question: You ___ have asked for permission before using the laboratory equipment.
activities.feedback: 'Should have + past participle' for past criticism/regret. The person didn't ask permission, and the speaker thinks they were wrong not to.

activities.question: ___ you mind opening the window? It's very hot.
activities.feedback: 'Would you mind + -ing?' is a polite request formula. 'Do you mind' is also correct but less formal. 'Would' adds politeness.

activities.question: He ___ to see a doctor about that cough. It's been going on for weeks.
activities.feedback: 'Ought to' = strong advice, slightly more formal than 'should.' Both are correct here, but 'ought to' implies moral/logical duty.

activities.question: When I was young, I ___ run 10 kilometers without stopping.
activities.feedback: 'Could' for general past ability. 'When I was young' signals past time. 'Can' is for present ability.

activities.question: You ___ be joking! There's no way the exam is tomorrow.
activities.feedback: 'Must be' for strong present deduction/logical conclusion. The speaker is almost certain the other person is joking.

activities.question: ___ I borrow your pen for a moment, please?
activities.feedback: 'May I' for formal/polite permission requests. 'Can I' is informal but widely accepted. 'Must I' asks about obligation, not permission.

activities.question: They ___ have arrived by now; the flight was scheduled for 3 PM.
activities.feedback: 'Should have + past participle' for expectation (not criticism here). Given the 3 PM schedule, we expect they've arrived. 'Must have' would be stronger certainty.

activities.question: If it ___ tomorrow, we will cancel the football match in Limbe.
activities.feedback: First conditional: 'If + present simple, will + base verb.' For real/likely future situations. Never use 'will' in the if-clause.

activities.question: If I ___ enough money, I would buy a car in Douala.
activities.feedback: Second conditional: 'If + past simple, would + base verb.' For unreal/hypothetical present situations. I don't have enough money (unreal).

activities.question: If you heat water to 100°C, it ___.
activities.feedback: Zero conditional: 'If + present simple, present simple.' For scientific facts and things that are always true.

activities.question: If she ___ the bus, she would have been late for the interview.
activities.feedback: Third conditional: 'If + past perfect, would have + past participle.' For unreal past situations. She didn't miss the bus (fortunately).

activities.question: Unless you ___ now, you'll miss the train to Kumba.
activities.feedback: 'Unless' = 'if...not.' Use present simple after 'unless' (like first conditional). 'Unless you leave' = 'If you don't leave.'

activities.question: I would travel the world if I ___ the lottery.
activities.feedback: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause for hypothetical situations. 'Won' doesn't mean past here — it signals unreality.

activities.question: If I ___ you, I would accept the job offer in Yaoundé.
activities.feedback: 'If I were you' uses subjunctive 'were' (not 'was') for all persons. This is a fixed expression for giving advice.

activities.question: What ___ if you found a wallet on the street in Bamenda?
activities.feedback: Second conditional question: 'What would you do if + past simple?' Hypothetical question about an imaginary situation.

activities.question: If you see my brother, ___ him I'm at the market.
activities.feedback: First conditional with imperative: 'If + present simple, imperative.' Used for instructions/requests about likely situations.

activities.question: As long as you ___ your best, the results don't matter.
activities.feedback: 'As long as' = 'on condition that.' Like first conditional: present simple for real conditions. 'As long as you do' = the condition is realistic.

activities.question: This song ___ by millions of people across Africa.
activities.feedback: Present simple passive: 'is/are + past participle.' The song receives the love (it doesn't love). Active: 'Millions of people love this song.'

activities.question: The thief ___ by the police near the Mokolo market yesterday.
activities.feedback: Past simple passive: 'was/were + past participle.' The thief received the action (was arrested by police).

activities.question: Over 200 languages ___ in Cameroon.
activities.feedback: Present simple passive for general facts. Languages don't speak themselves — they ARE SPOKEN by people.

activities.question: The new library ___ by the end of next year.
activities.feedback: Future simple passive: 'will + be + past participle.' The library receives the action of completion.

activities.question: The homework has already ___ by all the students.
activities.feedback: Present perfect passive: 'has/have + been + past participle.' 'Already' signals completion. The homework was submitted (it received the action).

activities.question: morning / every / she / school / to / walks
activities.answer: She walks to school every morning
acceptableAnswers: Every morning she walks to school
activities.feedback: In English, the basic word order is Subject + Verb + Object/Place + Time. 'She walks to school every morning' follows S-V-Place-Time order.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Elle marche à l'école chaque matin." (Same S-V-O-Time order in this case.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "She walks to school every morning." (S-V-Place-Time is standard.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: At this level, word order is similar. French speakers may place time adverbs first ('Every morning she walks...'), which is acceptable but less common in English.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Every morning walks she to school.
visualHighlighting.correct: She walks to school every morning.

activities.question: a / has / Douala / big / market
activities.feedback: In English, adjectives come BEFORE the noun: 'big market', not 'market big'.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Douala a un grand marché." (In French, 'grand' is one of the few adjectives placed before the noun.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Douala has a big market." (In English, adjectives always come before the noun.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: Most French adjectives follow the noun (une voiture rouge), but English adjectives always precede it. French speakers may write 'a market big' by habit.

activities.question: not / English / does / speak / he
activities.feedback: English negation requires the auxiliary 'does' + 'not' before the base verb: 'He does not speak.' The main verb stays in its base form.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Il ne parle pas anglais." (French wraps the verb with ne...pas.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "He does not speak English." (English uses does + not + base verb.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French negation surrounds the verb (ne...pas), so French speakers may write 'He not speaks English' or 'He speaks not English' instead of using the do-support.

activities.question: the / children / playing / are / in / park / the
activities.answer: The children are playing in the park
activities.feedback: Present Continuous uses Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing. 'The children are playing in the park.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Les enfants jouent dans le parc." (French uses simple present for actions happening now.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "The children are playing in the park." (English requires Present Continuous for current actions.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French has no direct equivalent of the Present Continuous. Speakers may write 'The children play in the park' when describing a current action.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: The children play in the park.
visualHighlighting.correct: The children are playing in the park.

activities.question: like / very / I / Bamenda / much
activities.feedback: 'Very much' comes after the object in English: 'I like Bamenda very much.' Not between the verb and object.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "J'aime beaucoup Bamenda." (In French, 'beaucoup' comes between the verb and the object.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "I like Bamenda very much." (In English, 'very much' goes after the object.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French places adverbs of degree between the verb and object (j'aime beaucoup...), so French speakers may write 'I like very much Bamenda.'

activities.question: you / where / yesterday / did / go
activities.feedback: English questions use: Question word + auxiliary + subject + verb. 'Where did you go yesterday?'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Où es-tu allé hier ?" or "Tu es allé où hier ?" (French allows multiple question structures.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Where did you go yesterday?" (English requires strict question word + did + subject + base verb order.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French allows informal questions without inversion ('Tu es allé où?'), so French speakers may write 'You did go where yesterday?' or 'Where you went yesterday?'

activities.question: never / she / been / has / to / Kribi
activities.answer: She has never been to Kribi
activities.feedback: Frequency adverbs like 'never' go between the auxiliary and the main verb: 'has never been.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Elle n'est jamais allée à Kribi." (French places 'jamais' as part of the ne...jamais negation around the auxiliary.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "She has never been to Kribi." (English places 'never' between auxiliary and past participle.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French negation (ne...jamais) surrounds the verb differently. Speakers may write 'She never has been' or 'She has been never to Kribi.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: She never has been to Kribi.
visualHighlighting.correct: She has never been to Kribi.

activities.question: gave / a / me / beautiful / Ngozi / necklace
activities.answer: Ngozi gave me a beautiful necklace
activities.feedback: With two objects, English puts the indirect object (me) before the direct object (a beautiful necklace): S + V + IO + DO.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Ngozi m'a donné un beau collier." (French uses a pronoun 'm'' before the verb for the indirect object.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Ngozi gave me a beautiful necklace." (English places the indirect object after the verb, before the direct object.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French places indirect object pronouns before the verb (m'a donné), but English places them after. French speakers may write 'Ngozi me gave a necklace.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Ngozi me gave a beautiful necklace.
visualHighlighting.correct: Ngozi gave me a beautiful necklace.

activities.question: enough / is / old / he / to / drive
activities.answer: He is old enough to drive
activities.feedback: 'Enough' comes AFTER adjectives in English: 'old enough', not 'enough old.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Il est assez vieux pour conduire." (French places 'assez' BEFORE the adjective.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "He is old enough to drive." (English places 'enough' AFTER the adjective.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'assez' comes before the adjective (assez vieux), but English 'enough' comes after (old enough). This reversal is a common source of errors.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: He is enough old to drive.
visualHighlighting.correct: He is old enough to drive.

activities.question: already / the / finished / students / have / exam / the
activities.answer: The students have already finished the exam
activities.feedback: 'Already' typically goes between the auxiliary and past participle: 'have already finished.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Les étudiants ont déjà fini l'examen." (French also places 'déjà' between auxiliary and past participle.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "The students have already finished the exam." (Same position in English.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: While the position is similar, French speakers sometimes place 'already' at the end ('have finished already the exam') or before the auxiliary.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: The students already have finished the exam.
visualHighlighting.correct: The students have already finished the exam.

activities.question: told / not / the / me / teacher / to / run
activities.answer: The teacher told me not to run
activities.feedback: In reported commands with negation, 'not' comes before 'to': 'told me not to run.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Le professeur m'a dit de ne pas courir." (French: de ne pas + infinitive.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "The teacher told me not to run." (English: not to + infinitive.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French places the negation within 'de ne pas + infinitif', while English uses 'not to + infinitive.' French speakers may write 'told me to not run.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: The teacher told me to not run.
visualHighlighting.correct: The teacher told me not to run.

activities.question: interesting / what / an / story
activities.feedback: English exclamations follow: 'What + a/an + adjective + noun!' The adjective comes before the noun.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Quelle histoire intéressante !" (French: Quelle + noun + adjective.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "What an interesting story!" (English: What + article + adjective + noun.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French exclamations place the adjective after the noun (histoire intéressante), and French 'Quel(le)' doesn't need an article. English requires 'a/an' + adjective before noun.

activities.question: carefully / she / the / read / letter / very
activities.answer: She read the letter very carefully
acceptableAnswers: She very carefully read the letter
activities.feedback: Adverbs of manner usually go at the end of the sentence: 'read the letter very carefully.' They can also go before the verb for emphasis.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Elle a lu la lettre très attentivement." (French also often places manner adverbs at the end.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "She read the letter very carefully." (Manner adverb at end is most natural.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French sometimes places short adverbs between auxiliary and participle (a bien lu). French speakers may put longer adverbs between verb and object in English.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: She read very carefully the letter.
visualHighlighting.correct: She read the letter very carefully.

activities.question: so / that / was / tired / he / he / slept / immediately
activities.answer: He was so tired that he slept immediately
activities.feedback: The 'so...that' construction follows: Subject + was + so + adjective + that + clause.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Il était tellement fatigué qu'il s'est endormi immédiatement." (French uses 'tellement...que'.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "He was so tired that he slept immediately." (English uses 'so...that'.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: The structure is similar, but French speakers may confuse 'so' with 'very' and omit the 'that' clause, or place 'so' incorrectly.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: He was tired so that he slept immediately.
visualHighlighting.correct: He was so tired that he slept immediately.

activities.question: neither / the / nor / father / mother / the / agreed
activities.answer: Neither the father nor the mother agreed
activities.feedback: 'Neither...nor' pairs two negative elements: 'Neither X nor Y + verb.'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Ni le père ni la mère n'a accepté." (French uses 'ni...ni' with 'ne' before the verb.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Neither the father nor the mother agreed." (English uses 'neither...nor' without extra negation.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French requires an additional 'ne' with 'ni...ni', so speakers may add double negation in English: 'Neither the father nor the mother didn't agree.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Neither the father nor the mother didn't agree.
visualHighlighting.correct: Neither the father nor the mother agreed.

activities.question: only / did / realize / then / I / my / mistake
activities.answer: Only then did I realize my mistake
activities.feedback: When 'only then' starts the sentence, English inverts the subject and auxiliary: 'Only then did I realize...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "C'est seulement alors que j'ai réalisé mon erreur." (French uses a cleft construction.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Only then did I realize my mistake." (English inverts subject and auxiliary after fronted adverbs.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: Subject-auxiliary inversion after negative/restrictive adverbs doesn't exist in French. Speakers may write 'Only then I did realize' or 'Only then I realized.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Only then I realized my mistake.
visualHighlighting.correct: Only then did I realize my mistake.

activities.question: had / known / I / if / would / I / have / come / earlier
activities.answer: If I had known I would have come earlier
acceptableAnswers: I would have come earlier if I had known
activities.feedback: Third conditional: 'If + past perfect, would + have + past participle.' Both clause orders are acceptable.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Si j'avais su, je serais venu plus tôt." (French uses si + plus-que-parfait, conditionnel passé.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "If I had known, I would have come earlier." (English uses if + past perfect, would have + past participle.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'si + plus-que-parfait' which maps differently. Speakers may confuse tense sequence or write 'If I would have known.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: If I would have known, I would have come.
visualHighlighting.correct: If I had known, I would have come earlier.

activities.question: not / should / only / have / apologized / he / but / also / paid / the / damage / for
activities.answer: Not only should he have apologized but also paid for the damage
activities.feedback: 'Not only' at the start triggers inversion: 'Not only should he have... but also...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Non seulement il aurait dû s'excuser, mais aussi payer les dégâts." (No inversion in French.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Not only should he have apologized, but also paid for the damage." (Inversion required after 'Not only'.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French doesn't invert after 'non seulement', so speakers write normal word order: 'Not only he should have apologized.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Not only he should have apologized.
visualHighlighting.correct: Not only should he have apologized.

activities.question: the / built / was / in / which / house / Yaoundé / 1985 / in / was
activities.answer: The house which was built in 1985 was in Yaoundé
activities.feedback: Relative clauses come directly after the noun they modify: 'The house which was built in 1985...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "La maison qui a été construite en 1985 était à Yaoundé." (French relative clauses also follow the noun.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "The house which was built in 1985 was in Yaoundé." (Same position.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: The double use of 'was' (relative clause + main verb) and 'in' (year + location) can confuse learners into placing elements in the wrong clause.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: The house was in Yaoundé which was built in 1985.
visualHighlighting.correct: The house which was built in 1985 was in Yaoundé.

activities.question: had / hardly / when / started / the / it / rain / match / to / began
activities.answer: Hardly had the match started when it began to rain
activities.feedback: 'Hardly' at the start triggers inversion: 'Hardly had + subject + past participle + when...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "À peine le match avait-il commencé qu'il s'est mis à pleuvoir." (French also inverts with 'à peine' but uses 'que' instead of 'when'.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Hardly had the match started when it began to rain." (Inversion with 'hardly...when'.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: While French also inverts with 'à peine', the connector differs (que vs when). Speakers may use 'that' instead of 'when' or avoid inversion entirely.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Hardly the match had started when it began to rain.
visualHighlighting.correct: Hardly had the match started when it began to rain.

activities.question: no / had / sooner / arrived / than / we / the / it / started / ceremony
activities.answer: No sooner had we arrived than the ceremony started
activities.feedback: 'No sooner' triggers inversion: 'No sooner had + subject + past participle + than...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "À peine étions-nous arrivés que la cérémonie a commencé." (French uses 'à peine...que'.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "No sooner had we arrived than the ceremony started." (English uses 'no sooner...than'.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: The 'no sooner...than' pattern requires inversion and 'than' (not 'that/when'). French speakers may write 'No sooner we had arrived that...'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: No sooner we had arrived that the ceremony started.
visualHighlighting.correct: No sooner had we arrived than the ceremony started.

activities.question: were / to / I / you / would / differently / I / things / do
activities.answer: Were I to do things differently I would
acceptableAnswers: Were I you I would do things differently
activities.feedback: Formal inverted conditionals drop 'if' and invert: 'Were I you, I would do things differently.' This is equivalent to 'If I were you...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Si j'étais toi, je ferais les choses différemment." (French never drops 'si' for inversion.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Were I you, I would do things differently." (Formal English drops 'if' and inverts.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: This inverted conditional structure doesn't exist in French. Speakers may not recognize it or may write 'If were I you...'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: If were I you, I would do things differently.
visualHighlighting.correct: Were I you, I would do things differently.

activities.question: under / should / circumstances / no / leave / you / building / the
activities.answer: Under no circumstances should you leave the building
activities.feedback: Negative adverbial phrases at the start trigger inversion: 'Under no circumstances should you...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "En aucun cas vous ne devez quitter le bâtiment." (French uses 'ne...aucun' without inversion.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Under no circumstances should you leave the building." (English inverts after fronted negative phrase.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French doesn't require inversion after negative adverbials. Speakers may write 'Under no circumstances you should leave...'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Under no circumstances you should leave the building.
visualHighlighting.correct: Under no circumstances should you leave the building.

activities.question: little / know / did / about / she / the / consequences
activities.answer: Little did she know about the consequences
