activities.feedback: 'Little' at the start with negative meaning triggers inversion: 'Little did she know...'

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Elle ne savait guère les conséquences." (French uses 'ne...guère' without inversion.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Little did she know about the consequences." (Inversion after 'Little'.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: This literary inversion pattern is unique to English. French speakers may write 'Little she knew about the consequences.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Little she knew about the consequences.
visualHighlighting.correct: Little did she know about the consequences.

activities.question: seldom / such / have / a / performance / I / seen / magnificent
activities.answer: Seldom have I seen such a magnificent performance
activities.feedback: 'Seldom' at the start triggers inversion: 'Seldom have I seen...' Note also 'such a + adjective + noun' word order.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: "Rarement ai-je vu un spectacle aussi magnifique." (French can invert with 'rarement' in formal register.)
frenchComparison.englishStructure: "Seldom have I seen such a magnificent performance." (Inversion required after 'seldom'.)
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: While French can use similar inversion with 'rarement', it's optional. In English it's obligatory. Speakers may also struggle with 'such a magnificent performance' vs 'a such magnificent performance.'

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Seldom I have seen a such magnificent performance.
visualHighlighting.correct: Seldom have I seen such a magnificent performance.

activities.question: Amina ___ (to go) to the market in Douala yesterday.
activities.feedback: The correct past tense of 'go' is 'went'. This is an irregular verb — there is no pattern like adding '-ed'. You must memorize it: go → went → gone.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Amina est allée au marché hier.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Amina went to the market yesterday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: In French, 'aller' uses 'être' as auxiliary in passé composé (est allée). English has no auxiliary — just the irregular past form 'went'. French speakers sometimes write 'goed' by applying regular -ed rules.

activities.question: She is interested ___ learning about Cameroonian history.
activities.feedback: In English, we say 'interested in' something. The preposition 'in' always follows 'interested' when talking about what attracts your attention.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle est intéressée par l'histoire camerounaise.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She is interested in Cameroonian history.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'intéressé par' (literally 'interested by'), so French speakers often write 'interested by' instead of the correct 'interested in'.

activities.question: I always arrive ___ school at 7:30 in Bamenda.
activities.feedback: We use 'at' for specific locations or buildings when talking about arrival. 'Arrive at school', 'arrive at the office', 'arrive at the station'.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: J'arrive à l'école à 7h30.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: I arrive at school at 7:30.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'à' for both time and place (arriver à l'école, à 7h30). English distinguishes: 'at school' (place) vs 'at 7:30' (time). French speakers sometimes write 'arrive to school' because 'à' can also mean 'to'.

activities.question: My uncle ___ (to buy) a new car last month in Yaoundé.
activities.feedback: The past tense of 'buy' is 'bought' — an irregular verb. Buy → bought → bought. The spelling changes completely, so memorization is essential.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Mon oncle a acheté une nouvelle voiture le mois dernier.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: My uncle bought a new car last month.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses a regular pattern (acheter → a acheté) with auxiliary 'avoir'. English 'buy → bought' is completely irregular with no auxiliary in simple past. French speakers may write 'buyed'.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: My uncle buyed a new car
visualHighlighting.correct: My uncle bought a new car

activities.question: Ngozi plays ___ piano every evening after dinner.
activities.feedback: In English, we always use 'the' before musical instruments: 'play the piano', 'play the guitar', 'play the drums'. This is a fixed rule.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Ngozi joue du piano chaque soir.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Ngozi plays the piano every evening.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses the partitive article 'du' (jouer du piano), while English requires the definite article 'the' (play the piano). The French 'du' has no direct English equivalent, leading to confusion about which article to use.

activities.question: The teacher told the students to ___ (to hand) in their homework by Friday.
activities.feedback: 'Hand in' means to submit or give something to someone in authority. 'Turn in' is also acceptable. These phrasal verbs are very common in school and work settings.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le professeur a dit aux élèves de remettre leurs devoirs avant vendredi.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The teacher told the students to hand in their homework by Friday.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses a single verb 'remettre' (to hand back/submit). English phrasal verbs like 'hand in' combine a simple verb with a particle that changes its meaning. French speakers often try to use a direct translation like 'give' instead of the phrasal verb.

activities.question: We have been living in Douala ___ 2015.
activities.feedback: Use 'since' with a specific point in time (2015, Monday, January). Use 'for' with a duration (5 years, 3 months, a long time).

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Nous vivons à Douala depuis 2015.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: We have been living in Douala since 2015.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'depuis' for both 'since' (point in time) and 'for' (duration): 'depuis 2015' and 'depuis 5 ans'. English strictly distinguishes them. French speakers often confuse 'since' and 'for'.

activities.question: She ___ (to make) a terrible mistake during the meeting in Yaoundé.
activities.feedback: In English, we say 'make a mistake', not 'do a mistake'. This is a fixed collocation you must memorize. Similarly: make a decision, make an effort, make progress.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle a fait une terrible erreur pendant la réunion.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She made a terrible mistake during the meeting.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'faire' (to do/make) for 'faire une erreur'. Since 'faire' translates to both 'do' and 'make', French speakers often say 'do a mistake' instead of 'make a mistake'. The make/do distinction does not exist in French.

activities.question: The children were excited ___ going on a trip to Kribi beach.
activities.feedback: 'Excited about' is the correct collocation. We use 'about' after 'excited' to introduce the cause of excitement.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les enfants étaient enthousiastes à l'idée d'aller à la plage de Kribi.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The children were excited about going on a trip to Kribi beach.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'enthousiasmé de/à l'idée de' — prepositions 'de' or 'à'. French speakers often write 'excited of' or 'excited to going' instead of 'excited about going'.

activities.question: He ___ (to catch) the bus just in time this morning.
activities.feedback: 'Catch' is irregular: catch → caught → caught. 'This morning' tells us the action is completed, so we use Past Simple.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Il a attrapé le bus juste à temps ce matin.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: He caught the bus just in time this morning.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'attraper' follows a regular conjugation pattern (a attrapé). English 'catch → caught' is highly irregular. French speakers may write 'catched' by applying the regular -ed ending.

activities.question: The project manager asked us to ___ up with a creative solution.
activities.feedback: 'Come up with' means to think of or produce an idea or solution. It is a three-part phrasal verb commonly used in professional settings.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le chef de projet nous a demandé de trouver une solution créative.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The project manager asked us to come up with a creative solution.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses the simple verb 'trouver' (to find). English uses a three-part phrasal verb 'come up with' whose meaning cannot be guessed from the individual words. French speakers tend to use 'find' instead of this more natural phrasal verb.

visualHighlighting.correct: to come up with a creative solution

activities.question: Despite ___ (to work) hard all semester, Fabrice failed the exam.
activities.feedback: After 'despite', we use a gerund (-ing form): 'despite working'. We can also say 'despite having worked'. Never use an infinitive after 'despite'.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Malgré avoir travaillé dur tout le semestre, Fabrice a échoué à l'examen.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Despite working hard all semester, Fabrice failed the exam.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses the infinitive after 'malgré' (malgré avoir travaillé). English requires a gerund after 'despite' (despite working). French speakers often write 'despite to work' following the French infinitive pattern.

activities.question: If I ___ (to know) about the ceremony in Bamenda, I would have attended.
activities.feedback: This is a third conditional (unreal past). The structure is: If + past perfect (had known), ... would have + past participle. It expresses regret about something that didn't happen.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Si j'avais su pour la cérémonie à Bamenda, j'y serais allé.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: If I had known about the ceremony, I would have attended.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses plus-que-parfait (si j'avais su) and conditionnel passé (j'y serais allé). While structurally similar, French speakers often simplify to 'If I knew' (second conditional) instead of 'If I had known' (third conditional), mixing up the two conditional forms.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: If I knew about the ceremony
visualHighlighting.correct: If I had known about the ceremony

activities.question: Sandrine depends ___ her older sister for school fees.
activities.feedback: 'Depend on' is the correct collocation. 'Depend upon' is also acceptable but more formal. Never say 'depend of'.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Sandrine dépend de sa grande sœur pour les frais de scolarité.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Sandrine depends on her older sister for school fees.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'dépendre de' (depend of). English uses 'depend on'. The French preposition 'de' typically translates to 'of', so French speakers frequently write 'depend of' instead of 'depend on'.

activities.question: The government ___ (to build) a new hospital in Douala by the end of next year.
activities.feedback: 'By the end of next year' signals the future perfect tense: will have + past participle. It describes an action that will be completed before a specific future time.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le gouvernement aura construit un nouvel hôpital à Douala d'ici la fin de l'année prochaine.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The government will have built a new hospital in Douala by the end of next year.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French future anterior (aura construit) is structurally simpler — two words vs. three in English (will have built). French speakers may write 'will build' (simple future) instead of 'will have built' (future perfect), missing the completed-before-deadline meaning.

visualHighlighting.correct: will have built a new hospital

activities.question: I need ___ (a/an) umbrella because it looks like rain.
activities.feedback: Use 'an' before words that start with a vowel sound: an umbrella, an apple, an hour. Use 'a' before consonant sounds: a car, a university.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: J'ai besoin d'un parapluie.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: I need an umbrella.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French does not change 'un/une' based on the following sound — the choice depends on gender (un parapluie, une voiture). English 'a/an' depends on the next sound, not gender. French speakers may write 'a umbrella' because they are not used to changing the article based on the following sound.

activities.question: The CEO insisted ___ reviewing every contract before signing.
activities.feedback: 'Insist on' + gerund (-ing) is the correct pattern. 'Insist upon' is also correct but more formal. You can also say 'insist that + subject + verb'.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Le PDG a insisté pour examiner chaque contrat avant de signer.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The CEO insisted on reviewing every contract before signing.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'insister pour + infinitif'. English uses 'insist on + gerund'. French speakers often write 'insisted to review' (following French infinitive pattern) instead of 'insisted on reviewing'.

activities.question: You had better ___ (to study) for the BEPC exam if you want to pass.
activities.feedback: 'Had better' is followed by the bare infinitive (without 'to'): had better study, had better leave, had better hurry. It expresses strong advice or warning.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Tu ferais mieux d'étudier pour le BEPC si tu veux réussir.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: You had better study for the BEPC exam if you want to pass.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'ferais mieux de + infinitif' where 'de' precedes the verb. English 'had better' takes a bare infinitive (no 'to'). French speakers often write 'had better to study' adding an unnecessary 'to'.

activities.question: Not only ___ she finish the report, but she also prepared the presentation.
activities.feedback: When 'Not only' starts a sentence, we must invert the subject and auxiliary verb: 'Not only did she finish...' This is called subject-auxiliary inversion for emphasis.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Non seulement elle a terminé le rapport, mais elle a aussi préparé la présentation.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Not only did she finish the report, but she also prepared the presentation.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French does not require inversion after 'non seulement' — the subject stays before the verb. English requires subject-auxiliary inversion after negative adverbs at the start of a sentence. French speakers write 'Not only she finished' without the inversion.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Not only she finished the report
visualHighlighting.correct: Not only did she finish the report

activities.question: The information you provided ___ (to be) very helpful for our research.
activities.feedback: 'Information' is an uncountable noun in English and takes a singular verb: 'the information was'. Never say 'informations' or 'the information were'. For plural, say 'pieces of information'.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Les informations que vous avez fournies étaient très utiles pour notre recherche.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: The information you provided was very helpful for our research.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French 'informations' is countable and commonly plural (les informations étaient). English 'information' is always uncountable and singular. French speakers write 'the informations were' applying French plural rules to an English uncountable noun.

activities.question: Had the rains not stopped, the Bamenda-Douala road ___ (to be) impassable.
activities.feedback: This is an inverted third conditional: 'Had + subject + past participle' replaces 'If + subject + had + past participle'. The main clause uses 'would have + past participle'. It is a formal, literary structure.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Si les pluies ne s'étaient pas arrêtées, la route Bamenda-Douala aurait été impraticable.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Had the rains not stopped, the road would have been impassable.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French does not have inverted conditionals — 'si' is always present. The English inverted form (Had ... not stopped) is a formal register shift that French speakers rarely encounter. They may also struggle with the three-part verb 'would have been'.

visualHighlighting.correct: the road would have been impassable

activities.question: Kofi can't help ___ (to laugh) whenever his friend tells that joke.
activities.feedback: 'Can't help' is followed by a gerund (-ing): 'can't help laughing' means 'cannot stop yourself from laughing'. The alternative 'can't help but laugh' (bare infinitive) is also correct.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Kofi ne peut pas s'empêcher de rire quand son ami raconte cette blague.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Kofi can't help laughing whenever his friend tells that joke.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 's'empêcher de + infinitif'. English uses 'can't help + gerund'. French speakers often write 'can't help to laugh' following the French 'de + infinitive' pattern instead of using the gerund.

activities.question: Rarely ___ such a talented musician perform at the Yaoundé Cultural Centre.
activities.feedback: When 'rarely' begins a sentence, subject-auxiliary inversion is required: 'Rarely does one see...' This formal structure adds emphasis and is common in written and academic English.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Rarement voit-on un musicien aussi talentueux se produire au Centre Culturel de Yaoundé.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Rarely does one see such a talented musician perform at the Yaoundé Cultural Centre.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: While French also has inversion after 'rarement' (voit-on), the English auxiliary 'does' must be inserted: 'Rarely does one see'. French speakers may omit the auxiliary and write 'Rarely one sees' or struggle with which auxiliary to use.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Rarely one sees such a talented musician
visualHighlighting.correct: Rarely does one see such a talented musician

activities.question: She ___ (to look) forward to starting her new job in Douala.
activities.feedback: 'Look forward to' is a phrasal verb where 'to' is a preposition, not part of an infinitive. It must be followed by a gerund: 'look forward to starting' (not 'to start').

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: Elle a hâte de commencer son nouveau travail à Douala.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: She is looking forward to starting her new job in Douala.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French uses 'avoir hâte de + infinitif'. In English, 'look forward to' requires a gerund because 'to' here is a preposition. French speakers write 'look forward to start' treating 'to' as the infinitive marker, which is the most common error with this phrasal verb.

activities.question: Under no circumstances ___ employees allowed to leave the Douala factory without authorization.
activities.feedback: When 'under no circumstances' starts a sentence, subject-auxiliary inversion is required: 'Under no circumstances are employees allowed...' This formal structure emphasizes the prohibition.

frenchComparison.frenchStructure: En aucun cas les employés ne sont autorisés à quitter l'usine de Douala sans autorisation.
frenchComparison.englishStructure: Under no circumstances are employees allowed to leave the factory without authorization.
frenchComparison.whyDifficult: French places the subject before the verb after 'en aucun cas' (les employés ne sont). English inverts the auxiliary and subject (are employees). French speakers tend to write 'Under no circumstances employees are allowed' keeping normal word order.

visualHighlighting.incorrect: Under no circumstances employees are allowed
visualHighlighting.correct: Under no circumstances are employees allowed
